summary:
Elevated prolactin can be seen in cases of Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome (PCOS), though not typically a defining feature; thus, its presence does not exclude diagnosis from being correct under most conditions unless significantly high or clinically symptomatic.
All statements regarding PCOS should consider these nuances when evaluating patient presentations. The exception here would likely refer specifically to scenarios requiring higher specificity than what typical variations allow within normal ranges or non-PCOS related elevations. To directly answer your query succinctly while adhering strictly to requirements without extraneous details,
The statement "all others" implies an incorrect exclusion if referring solely based on isolated abnormality rather than comprehensive assessment including hormonal ratios specific to HPG function disruptions central to diagnosing PCOS accurately. ```plaintext
Elevated prolactin may sometimes coexist with PCOS but isn't a primary criterion; hence, finding raised prolactin shouldn't definitively rule out PCOS unless markedly increased. 
Therefore, all stated facts hold until encountering abnormally high prolactin unrelated to underlying endocrine imbalances characterizing PCOS.
```
